concepts.title: Primality testing
concepts.blurb: Fermat's little theorem, the Miller–Rabin probabilistic test, and the AKS deterministic polynomial-time algorithm. Composite witnesses turn $a^{n-1}\not\equiv 1\pmod n$ into a verifiable proof of compositeness; AKS removes randomness at the cost of a higher polynomial.

concepts.title: Integer factoring
concepts.blurb: Trial division, Pollard's $\rho$ method (Floyd cycle detection on $x \mapsto x^2+1 \bmod n$), Pollard's $p-1$ method, the quadratic sieve, and the general number-field sieve. Subexponential complexity $L_n[1/3,c]$ is the current frontier — no polynomial-time classical algorithm is known.

concepts.title: LLL & lattice reduction
concepts.blurb: The Lenstra–Lenstra–Lovász algorithm finds a basis whose first vector is at most $2^{(n-1)/2}$ times the shortest in polynomial time. Applications: integer-relation detection (PSLQ-style), Coppersmith's small-roots attack on RSA, knapsack cryptanalysis, and post-quantum lattice schemes.

concepts.title: Modular arithmetic algorithms
concepts.blurb: Fast modular exponentiation by repeated squaring ($O(\log n)$ multiplies), Tonelli–Shanks for square roots modulo $p$ when $\left(\tfrac{a}{p}\right)=1$, and the Chinese remainder theorem implemented as Garner's algorithm. The arithmetic primitives every higher-level routine assumes.

concepts.title: Schoof & point counting
concepts.blurb: Schoof's algorithm computes $\#E(\mathbb{F}_p) = p+1-t$ in time polynomial in $\log p$ by determining $t \bmod \ell$ for many small primes $\ell$ via the action of Frobenius on $\ell$-torsion, then reconstructing $t$ via CRT. Schoof–Elkies–Atkin (SEA) speeds this up using modular polynomials.

concepts.title: Class & unit group computation
concepts.blurb: Buchmann's subexponential algorithm (under GRH) computes the class number, regulator, and a system of fundamental units of a number field by relation-collection in a factor base of small prime ideals plus LLL reduction of the relation lattice. The algorithm's correctness assumes the generalized Riemann hypothesis for Hecke L-functions.
